items.q: Why is 1688 called the Glorious Revolution?
items.correct: Parliament invited William of Orange to replace the Catholic James II, with little bloodshed in England, establishing parliamentary supremacy and the 1689 Bill of Rights.
items.misconception: It was glorious because no one died anywhere in the conflict.
items.random_wrong: William of Orange was Charles II's son and inherited the throne.
items.why: It was bloody in Ireland and Scotland (Boyne, Killiecrankie). Students take the English-centric label literally and miss the violent imposition of the new regime elsewhere.

items.q: What caused the First World War?
items.correct: Long-term tensions (militarism, alliances, imperialism, nationalism) combined with the July Crisis triggered by the assassination of Franz Ferdinand.
items.misconception: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand caused the war.
items.random_wrong: Germany declared war on Britain to seize colonies.
items.why: The assassination was the trigger, not the cause. Students treat the spark as sufficient explanation, ignoring the systemic pressures (MAIN factors) that made a wider war possible.

items.q: Why did the Communists win the Chinese Civil War?
items.correct: Mao's appeal to peasants through land reform, the discrediting of the KMT by corruption and inflation, and the disciplined organisation of the PLA — not foreign aid.
items.misconception: Mao won because the USSR sent the Red Army to help.
items.random_wrong: Mao won because the USA refused to support the KMT.
items.why: Soviet aid was limited and often unhelpful; the US heavily backed the KMT. The Communist victory was won domestically through rural mobilisation — a key historiographical point.

items.q: Who wrote The Making of the English Working Class (1963)?
items.why: It's E.P. Thompson. “Christopher Hill” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which school founded by Bloch and Febvre in 1929 emphasised structures?
items.why: It's Annales. “Frankfurt School” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which year was the Annales journal founded?
items.why: Exact answer: 1929. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
